source_label: Mastering Lightning Network — Chapter 10: Onchain Transaction Anatomy
excerpt: Cooperative close produces a single transaction with two outputs paying the current balances. Force close produces a commitment transaction whose layout depends on the channel state at the time of closure: the broadcasting party's main output is delayed, the counterparty's main output is immediate, and any in-flight HTLC outputs require additional second-stage transactions to claim or refund.
body:
Source excerpt:
Cooperative close produces a single transaction with two outputs paying the current balances. Force close produces a commitment transaction whose layout depends on the channel state at the time of closure: the broadcasting party's main output is delayed, the counterparty's main output is immediate, and any in-flight HTLC outputs require additional second-stage transactions to claim or refund.

---

Cited by leaf "Splicing, fees, and operational realities":
A Lightning channel is a long-lived relationship, and the operational details — when to open, when to close, when to rebalance, what fees to charge — make the difference between a node that earns routing fees and a node whose capital sits idle. Channel *opening* is straightforward: a single on-chain transaction creates a 2-of-2 funding output. The capital is then committed for the lifetime of the channel; the only way to extract it is to close the channel back to the chain. Open-cost is the on-chain fee at the time of funding; this is the dominant economic constraint on channel sizing and is the reason small channels are economically marginal at any non-trivial fee rate [1].

*Splicing* (BOLT-2 `update_splice`, recently activated in production by several implementations) lets the parties add or remove capital from an existing channel via a single on-chain transaction without closing it. Before splicing, a node that wanted to add liquidity had to close the existing channel and open a new larger one — two on-chain transactions plus a window during which the channel was unroutable. After splicing, capital can be added in-place while the channel keeps forwarding payments. This is a substantial UX and capital-efficiency win, especially for LSP architectures that need to dynamically resize channels in response to user demand [2].

Channel *closing* has two flavours. *Cooperative close* — both parties agree on the final state and exchange a single mutually-signed transaction paying out the current balances. This is the cheapest and fastest path: one on-chain transaction, no timelocks, both outputs are spendable immediately. *Force close* — one party publishes the most recent commitment transaction unilaterally. The publishing party's main output is locked behind the `to_self_delay` (typically 144 to 1008 blocks) before it can be spent; the counterparty can spend immediately. Any HTLCs in flight at force-close time get cleaned up by HTLC-timeout or HTLC-success transactions, each subject to its own timelock. Force close is more expensive and slower than coop close, but it's the safety net that makes Lightning work when peers are unresponsive [3].

Forwarding fees are the income side. A routing node sets two values on each channel direction: a `base_fee_msat` (a flat sat-millisat charged per HTLC routed, regardless of size) and a `fee_rate_milli_msat` (a proportional fee in parts-per-million of the amount forwarded). The fees are asymmetric and per-direction — outbound fees on this channel apply only when this node is the *forwarding* hop, not when it is the source or destination of a payment. Tuning fees against expected demand and against the fees of nearby nodes is treated by the major routing operators as a continuous optimisation problem; tools like `charge-lnd` and Lightning Terminal automate part of it.

Channel *reliability* — keeping the peer online, monitoring HTLC stuck states, watching for counterparty force-close attempts, fee-bumping the on-chain anchor outputs when network fees spike — is operational work that small nodes typically delegate to LSP-provided turnkey runtimes (Voltage, Galoy Greenlight) or run on a dedicated home node like Umbrel or Start9. Routing-as-a-business at scale is a real engineering job; routing-as-a-hobby on a Raspberry Pi is a real possibility but with materially lower expected returns once on-chain fees are netted against forwarding income.
